name: Xipe Totec
description: Xipe Totec, meaning 'Our Lord the Flayed One,' was a prominent deity in the pantheons of pre-Columbian Mesoamerica, particularly revered by the Nahua peoples, including the Aztecs. He embodies renewal, agriculture, the east, and goldsmithing. Xipe Totec's distinctive iconography involves him being depicted wearing the flayed skin of a sacrificial victim, symbolizing the shedding of the old to make way for the new. This act of flaying represented the earth shedding its old, dry skin to allow for new growth, analogous to a seed germinating after the outer layer has decayed. He was a complex figure, associated with both the horrors of sacrifice and the life-giving forces of nature. The rituals dedicated to him, most notably Tlacaxipehualiztli, were crucial for ensuring successful harvests and the continuation of life. His influence extended throughout Mesoamerica, with evidence of his worship found in various cultures and regions, highlighting his significance in the religious landscape.
subtitle: Our Lord the Flayed One

keyMyths.title: The Origin of Flaying
keyMyths.description: According to certain Aztec accounts, Xipe Totec flayed himself to provide food for humanity. This act of self-sacrifice demonstrates his profound commitment to the well-being of his people. The flayed skin is then worn by priests, imitating Xipe Totec and symbolizing the renewal of the earth after the dry season. This myth underscores the importance of sacrifice in maintaining cosmic balance and ensuring the continuation of life, reflecting a central theme in Mesoamerican religious thought.
keyMyths.source: Sahagún, Bernardino de. *General History of the Things of New Spain*.

keyMyths.title: The Festival of Tlacaxipehualiztli
keyMyths.description: Tlacaxipehualiztli, the festival of Xipe Totec, was a major event in the Aztec calendar. During this festival, captives were sacrificed and their skins were carefully removed and worn by priests for twenty days. These priests, impersonating Xipe Totec, would go through the city, receiving gifts and blessings. At the end of the twenty days, the flayed skins were buried, symbolizing the planting of new seeds and the cycle of life and death.
keyMyths.source: Durán, Diego. *The History of the Indies of New Spain*.

keyMyths.title: Xipe Totec and Warfare
keyMyths.description: Xipe Totec was also associated with warfare. Warriors who displayed exceptional bravery were sometimes flayed after death and their skins were worn by others, symbolizing the transfer of their strength and courage. This association highlights the dual nature of Xipe Totec as both a god of renewal and a god of war, reflecting the interconnectedness of life, death, and sacrifice in Mesoamerican cosmology. He was seen as a patron of warriors and a symbol of their resilience.
keyMyths.source: Boone, Elizabeth Hill. *Cycles of Time and Meaning in the Mexican Books of Fate*.

associations.name: Gold
associations.significance: Represents the sun, wealth, and the precious metal worked by goldsmiths, a craft associated with Xipe Totec.

associations.name: Earth
associations.significance: Connects to agriculture and the renewal of the land, as Xipe Totec is a deity of planting and harvest.

associations.name: East
associations.significance: The direction of new beginnings, rebirth, and the rising sun, aligning with Xipe Totec's symbolism of renewal.

associations.name: Sacrifice
associations.significance: Central to his mythology, representing the shedding of the old (skin) to allow for new life and growth.

associations.name: Renewal
associations.significance: The core essence of Xipe Totec, symbolizing the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth in agriculture and existence.

associations.name: Snake
associations.significance: Represents shedding of skin and rebirth, mirroring Xipe Totec's association with renewal.

worshipPractices.name: Human Sacrifice
worshipPractices.description: Victims, often war captives, were sacrificed, and their skin was flayed. A priest would then wear the flayed skin for a period, impersonating Xipe Totec. This act symbolized the deity's renewal and the shedding of the old to make way for the new.

worshipPractices.name: Offering of Seeds and Crops
worshipPractices.description: Farmers offered seeds, first fruits, and other agricultural products to Xipe Totec to ensure a bountiful harvest. These offerings were meant to appease the deity and secure his favor for fertile lands.

festivals.name: Tlacaxipehualiztli
festivals.timing: Second month of the Aztec calendar (approximately February/March)
festivals.description: The most important festival dedicated to Xipe Totec, meaning 'flaying of men'. It involved the sacrifice of war captives, the flaying of their skin, and priests wearing the skins for twenty days. This ritual was performed to ensure a successful planting season and abundant crops.

cultural.modernLegacy: Xipe Totec's imagery and symbolism continue to inspire contemporary artists and cultural movements in Mexico. He represents themes of sacrifice, renewal, and the cyclical nature of life, resonating with modern interpretations of indigenous identity and spirituality. His story is often retold in educational settings to preserve knowledge of Aztec culture.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Xipe Totec's origins trace back to earlier Mesoamerican cultures, with evidence of similar flayed god figures appearing in the Teotihuacan civilization. He was later adopted and integrated into the Nahua pantheon, becoming a prominent deity associated with agriculture, war, and renewal. While specific myths detailing his birth are scarce, his emergence likely stems from the agricultural cycle itself – the planting of seeds (a form of sacrifice) leading to new growth. Some accounts suggest connections to the earth goddess, solidifying his role as a vital force for fertility and sustenance. His association with goldsmiths might indicate a link to the earth's hidden treasures and the transformative power of craftsmanship, mirroring the skin-shedding transformation he embodies.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: While Xipe Totec lacks extensive, detailed narratives compared to some other Mesoamerican deities, his core myth revolves around the act of self-sacrifice. Legend states that Xipe Totec flayed himself to provide food for humanity, symbolizing the ultimate act of giving. This self-flaying is not portrayed as a singular event but as a continuous process of shedding the old to make way for the new. He is also associated with warfare and the renewal of warriors. Captives sacrificed to him were seen as seeds planted in the earth to ensure future victories and prosperity. These sacrifices were not viewed solely as acts of brutality but as necessary components of the cyclical nature of life and the continuation of the cosmos.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Xipe Totec's worship was characterized by dramatic and symbolic rituals. The most prominent was the Tlacaxipehualiztli festival, where war captives were sacrificed, and their skin was flayed. Priests, embodying Xipe Totec, would wear these skins for twenty days, representing the deity's regenerative power. These 'living gods' were venerated and offered gifts. The flayed skins, called *teocuitlaquemitl* ('golden garments'), were often adorned with jewelry and treated with reverence. Beyond human sacrifice, offerings of seeds, crops, and crafted objects were also made to Xipe Totec. His temples were centers for agricultural rites, and his priests played a crucial role in ensuring the success of planting and harvesting seasons. The cult of Xipe Totec was widespread throughout Mesoamerica, particularly among the Nahua peoples.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Xipe Totec's influence extended beyond his immediate cult following, shaping broader Mesoamerican concepts of sacrifice, renewal, and the cyclical nature of time. His imagery and symbolism permeated art, architecture, and religious practices. The flayed skin motif, though unsettling, served as a potent reminder of the constant transformation inherent in the universe. In modern times, Xipe Totec serves as a powerful symbol of indigenous identity and cultural resilience. His story is retold in educational settings and inspires contemporary artists seeking to reconnect with their ancestral heritage. He represents the complex and nuanced worldview of the ancient Mesoamericans, where death and sacrifice were viewed as essential components of life and renewal. His legacy serves as a reminder of the enduring power of ancient myths and their ability to resonate across centuries.

shortDescription: Xipe Totec, 'Our Lord the Flayed One,' is an Aztec deity of renewal, agriculture, and goldsmithing, symbolized by wearing the flayed skin of sacrificial victims.
